title: Meaning-prior reconstruction candidate
notes: Twenty-fifth-round mutation generated after depth-charge semantic illusions showed that normalized repair distance is still too surface-oriented. It tests whether intended-meaning plausibility and literal-composition coherence must be separated for semantic noisy-channel cases. It is unscored and not a scoped module.

edges.rationale: A plausible reconstructed intended meaning increases recoverability even when the literal composition is incoherent.
A plausible intended meaning can make a defective or incoherent literal string feel more natural on first pass.
Higher literal compositional coherence tends to increase felt naturalness when respondents attend to the presented string.
Literal semantic coherence can provide contextual attribution evidence in literal-analysis tasks, without defining grammar.
Recovering a plausible intended meaning raises felt naturalness.
Felt naturalness can surface as reported acceptability in first-pass tasks.
Task format changes whether respondents report the reconstructed intended meaning or the literal compositional status.
Task framing changes whether semantic anomaly, reconstructed meaning, or literal-string status is treated as grammar-relevant.
Community licensing remains attribution evidence, but semantic reconstruction is not licensing.
Reported acceptability is contextual attribution evidence and can be inflated by intended-meaning plausibility.
